clue: This title, despite being the most common word used to describe Jesus in Christian history, is the least used by Jesus himself in the four Gospels.
This prophetic book of the Hebrew Bible contains the strange dream from which Jesus drew his favored title, 'the Son of Man.'
This group title was used in the Hebrew Bible to designate the prophetic disciples in training under both Elijah and Elisha.
answer: What is "**the Sons of the Prophets**"?

clue: This familial Hebrew phrase uses gendered imagery to describe the satellite towns and suburban settlements surrounding Zion.
This apocalyptic figure, according to Jesus in Mark 8, must suffer and be rejected by the elders after Peter calls Jesus the Messiah.
This religious figure assumed the functional leadership role of king in Jesus's day because no Kingdom of Israel existed.
These powerful human political entities are represented in Daniel's vision by terrifying mutant beasts.
This divine figure is the ancient title given to the one who occupies the central throne in Daniel 7.
This sacred space of the temple, entered by the high priest, is what Daniel visually beholds when viewing the heavenly cosmic mountain.
This vehicle of divine transportation carries the Son of Man up to the Ancient of Days in Daniel's night vision.
This ancient Jewish book features a vision echoing Daniel 7, in which the kings of the earth fall down and worship the Son of Man.
answer: What is **1 Enoch (or the book of Enoch)**?

clue: This religious figure, himself an anointed leader, questions Jesus during his trial in Mark 14.
This element of judgment is where the fourth oppressive beast is cast after the divine court convenes in Daniel 7.
This execution device is what Jesus paradoxically refers to as being 'lifted up' to his throne in the Gospel of John.
These creatures represent what humans tragically devolve into when they reject God's rule and usurp divine authority.
This visionary prophet in Babylon sees a sequence of beastly empires contrasting with the Son of Man ascending to God's throne.
This divine seat remains unoccupied next to the Ancient of Days in Daniel 7, waiting for a human partner to share in cosmic rule.
This non-violent dietary lifestyle characterizes the ideal harmony between humans and animals in the Garden of Eden.
This field creature ironically deceives and rules over humanity in Genesis 3, subverting humanity's calling to rule over the earth.
This object of worship is a redundant oxymoron in the biblical worldview because humans were created to be God's living representations.
This apocalyptic figure is seen seated on the divine throne alongside God in Daniel 7, representing humanity exalted in God's image.
This Old Testament book contains instructions forbidding Israel's kings from accumulating mass wealth and military horses.
This biblical figure famously traded his glorious birthright for a bowl of porridge, illustrating humanity exchanging their divine vocation for lesser desires.
This human practice of cultivating the earth is what the Genesis command to 'subdue the land' specifically refers to.
This eternal figure occupies the single occupied throne in Daniel's dream of multiple heavenly seats.
This sacred chest in the Holy of Holies is the earthly counterpart to what Daniel beholds in his heavenly vision.
answer: What is **the Ark of the Covenant**?

clue: This human-like figure in Daniel 7 rides upon the clouds to be elevated to the right hand of God.
This biblical patriarch is guided through the heavens by angels in an ancient Second Temple book featuring a vision of the Son of Man.
This heavenly assembly is seated in judicial session when the books are unsealed in Daniel's night vision.
This Jewish leader unknowingly plays the role of the persecuting beast from Daniel 7 during Jesus's trial.
This wooden execution device is paradoxically described in John's Gospel as the altar of Jesus's royal ascension.
This exile prophet from the royal line of David experiences a terrifying dream of four mutant beasts emerging from the sea.
These two Old Testament prophets led training guilds of disciples known by the Hebrew idiom 'Sons of the Prophet.'
This lead disciple speaks up in Mark 8 to confess Jesus as the Messiah before Jesus reveals the Son of Man must suffer.
This holy city is described in Hebrew idiom as having surrounding suburban villages called its 'daughters.'
This self-designation of Jesus is noticeably absent from the letters of Paul, James, and Peter in the New Testament.
These terrifying sea creatures represent oppressive human empires that trample the earth in Daniel's vision.
This self-designation, drawn from Daniel 7, is Jesus's most frequent title for himself in the Gospels.
These wild creatures represent world empires trampling human dignity in Daniel's apocalyptic dream.
This firstborn son of Adam succumbs to an animalistic urge and slays his righteous brother in Genesis 4.
This son of Noah received a curse after mocking his father, becoming the ancestor of Nimrod and Canaan.
This ancient warrior is described as the first mighty hunter and beast slayer who built the original city of Babylon.
This firstborn son of Abraham is described with animal imagery as a 'wild donkey of a human.'
This son of Isaac comes in ravenous from the field and trades his birthright for a bowl of red stew.
This patriarch received a name meaning 'heel grabber' after emerging from the womb grasping his twin brother's heel.
This matriarch receives a divine revelation that two nations are in her womb and the elder will serve the younger.
This wooden staff is cast to the ground by Moses at God's command, instantly transforming into a serpent.
This aquatic monster of ancient mythology is used in Job and Psalms to symbolize chaotic forces opposed to God.
This brother of Moses serves as his spokesman, casts down his staff before Pharaoh, and becomes Israel's first high priest.
This ancient ruler of Egypt is compared to a chaotic sea monster in Ezekiel's prophetic lamentation.
This royal prophet in exile is presented as a 'Son of Man' figure who suffers persecution before being exalted in Babylon.
This Babylonian emperor looted the Jerusalem temple and carried the royal seed of Judah into exile in the 6th century BC.
This ancient Mesopotamian region, where the Tower of Babel stood, is where Nebuchadnezzar brought sacred temple vessels.
This capital city of the Neo-Assyrian empire lay along the Tigris river prior to the rise of Babylon.
This ancient empire succeeded Babylon and issued a decree permitting exiled peoples to return to their homelands.
This symbolic height, representing God's eternal kingdom, grows from the meteor that shatters Nebuchadnezzar's statue.
These three faithful Israelites survived being thrown into a roaring furnace after refusing to bow to Nebuchadnezzar's golden image.
This exalted human figure rides up on heavenly clouds in Daniel 7 to share in God's divine throne.
This Babylonian king was reigning when Daniel experienced his terrifying vision of four mutant beasts in Daniel 7.
These earthly rulers are represented by the ten horns on the terrifying fourth beast in Daniel's dream.
This Canaanite storm god was designated as the 'cloud rider' in ancient Near Eastern mythology.
This divine figure takes his seat upon a throne of fire in Daniel 7, wearing garments white as snow.
This Greek deity was forced upon the Jerusalem temple when Antiochus IV desecrated the sanctuary in 167 BC.
This Seleucid king outlawed Jewish worship in 167 BC, triggering a major crisis in Judea.
This Jewish rebellion erupted in response to Antiochus IV desecrating the temple and outlawing the Torah.
This Roman client king ruled over Judea during Jesus's birth and acted as a persecuting beast by slaying infants.
This Gospel narrative vividly describes Jesus being driven into the wilderness to be tested while dwelling with wild beasts.
This New Testament apostle repeatedly uses the term 'glorification' to describe believers being transformed into God's image.
This Pauline epistle compares Christ's light shining through human weakness to treasure inside fragile clay jars.
This Greek conqueror built a vast empire that split into warring northern and southern kingdoms after his death.
This prophetic book of the Hebrew Bible introduces the visionary benchmark for the title 'Son of Man.'
This Hebrew patriarch was exalted to rule in Egypt after enduring suffering and false imprisonment.
This miraculous wilderness food provided in Exodus is described as having the luster of Edenic precious stones.
This righteous patriarch, along with Adam and Daniel, is depicted as coexisting in peace with animals aboard the ark.
This holy city on Mount Zion is designated in Psalm 2 as the place where God establishes His anointed king.
This lead apostle took Jesus aside in Matthew 16 to rebuke him for teaching that the Messiah must suffer.
This ancient empire experienced ten devastating plagues when Pharaoh refused to release Israel.
This patriarch persevered through ten trials of faith, contrasting with Israel's failure in the wilderness.
This desolate region is where Jesus fasted for 40 days while undergoing temptation by the Satan.
These heavenly messengers ministered to Jesus while he endured testing among the wild beasts.
This Old Testament Torah book records that Israel's journey from Sinai to the promised land took 40 years due to rebellion.
These two royal attributes are what God crowns humanity with in Psalm 8 when delegating dominion over creation.
These angelic guardians were stationed east of Eden with flaming swords to guard the way to the tree of life.
This wilderness prophet is introduced in Mark's Gospel as the voice crying in the desert to prepare the Lord's way.
This Israelite king received a divine covenant in 2 Samuel 7 promising that his royal throne would endure forever.
This legendary weapon was retrieved by David from the priest at Nob while fleeing from Saul into the wilderness.
This oppressive empire is handed the cup of divine wrath in Jeremiah 25 for devastating the nations.
This ancient region, famous as the homeland of Job, is listed in Jeremiah 25 among those drinking the cup of wrath.
This Babylonian monarch is called God's servant in Jeremiah for executing judgment against rebellious kingdoms.
These two ambitious sons of Zebedee requested the chief places of honor on Jesus's right and left in his kingdom.
These two outcast individuals were unexpectedly positioned on Jesus's right and left when he was elevated on the cross.
These two rulers, representing religious and political authorities, collaborated to sentence Jesus to death.
These religious authorities accused Jesus of blasphemy when he claimed authority to forgive the paralytic's sins.
This foundational ancestor of humanity is who Jesus claims the Sabbath was originally instituted for in Mark 2.
This pristine garden sanctuary is what the disciples' grain-picking on the Sabbath is structurally linked to.
This holy city is where Jesus journeyed to confront the beastly powers and offer his life on the cross.
